title: Mark 11
subtitle: Triumphal Entry; Fig Tree; Temple Cleansed; Authority Questioned

sections.header: Verses 1–11 — The Triumphal Entry into Jerusalem

heb.paragraph: Hosanna — from the Hebrew hoshiʻa-na: “save now!” It is a cry from Psalm 118:25. What began as a liturgical plea for divine rescue became a shout of messianic welcome. The crowd is greeting the one they believe will bring salvation — but their understanding of what salvation means will be exposed within a week.

places.name: Bethphage and Bethany
places.coords: Villages on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives, c.3km from Jerusalem
places.text: The entry begins on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives — the traditional approach to Jerusalem from Jericho. Zechariah 14:4 locates the Messiah’s coming on the Mount of Olives. Jesus’s deliberate use of this route is a geographical claim.

places.name: Mount of Olives
places.coords: Hill east of Jerusalem, overlooking the Temple mount across the Kidron Valley
places.text: The Mount of Olives commands a view of the Temple courts. Jesus’s approach from the east, down the olive-covered slope, into the Kidron Valley and up to the Temple, is the route of royal processional. The geography makes the claim visible.

places.name: Jerusalem — Temple courts
places.coords: The city and its central sanctuary
places.text: Jesus enters the city and goes directly to the Temple — not to Herod’s palace, not to Pilate’s praetorium, but to the house of prayer. The Temple is his destination and his challenge.

tl.name: Triumphal Entry — Palm Sunday
tl.text: Jesus enters Jerusalem on a colt, acclaimed with Psalm 118. He surveys the Temple and withdraws to Bethany. The evaluation has begun.

refs.note: My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations — the text Jesus quotes to define the Temple’s purpose.
Has this house, which bears my Name, become a den of robbers? — Jeremiah’s Temple sermon that Jesus invokes.
What misery is mine! I am like one who gathers summer fruit… not a cluster of grapes… no figs — the OT background for the fruitless fig tree as a prophetic image of covenant failure.

mar.source: Joel Marcus,Mark 1–8; Mark 8–16(Word Biblical Commentary) — Faithful Paraphrase

notes.note: Joel Marcus (Word Biblical Commentary) notes that the preparation for the entry is structured as a royal requisition — the disciples' password-like exchange ("The Lord needs it") echoes the right of a king to commandeer property for state use (cf. 1 Sam 8:11-18). This is not simply logistical but theological: Jesus approaches Jerusalem as a king approaches his capital, and his arrival cannot be refused. The detail that the colt has never been ridden (v2) marks it as ritually fit for sacred use, parallel to the unbroken heifer of Numbers 19.

Marcus notes the deliberate restraint of Mark's entry narrative compared to later Christian readings: the crowd acclaims Jesus using Psalm 118 language, but the psalm is a pilgrimage song, not necessarily a messianic proclamation. The cry "Blessed is the coming kingdom of our father David!" (v10) is Mark's unique phrasing — the kingdom, not just the king, is announced. Marcus argues that the entry is best read as a prophetic sign-act, not a triumphal march: its significance is enacted, not explained, and the explanation comes only in retrospect through the passion.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: The fig tree / Temple / fig tree intercalation is the most explicitly structural of Mark's sandwiches. The two halves of the fig tree story (11:12-14, 20-25) bracket the Temple cleansing (11:15-19). Rhoads's interpretive principle: the two parts of the sandwich are meant to be read through each other. The fig tree's judgment (withering from the roots) is the Temple's judgment. The Temple cleansing is the fig tree's leaves being stripped. The withering visible the next morning is the Temple's visible coming fall.

Rhoads: The authority challenge is the first of five controversy exchanges in chs.11-12. The narrator characterizes the chief priests, scribes, and elders as a bloc rather than as individuals — they speak with a collective voice, deliberate collectively, and are collectively silenced. This group characterization distances them from the "one of the teachers of the law" in 12:28-34 who is treated as an individual and commended. The narrative distinguishes between the institutional establishment and the person within it who seeks honestly.

cat.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: The instruction about the colt (vv.1-6) is, for Chrysostom, a display of divine foreknowledge: the animal has never been ridden and awaits precisely this use. Origen notes that the disciples' readiness to answer "The Lord needs it" is itself a model of apostolic obedience — no extended explanation, just the word of the Lord and the expectation of compliance. The literal fulfilment of Zechariah 9:9 is, for Bede, the first public signal that Jesus is acting with full messianic intentionality.

The spreading of cloaks on the road is, for Theophylact, a royal carpet ceremony (cf. 2 Kings 9:13 for Jehu). The Hosanna cry from Psalm 118:25-26 is, for Chrysostom, both a petition and an acclamation — the crowd is crying for salvation to the very one who is bringing it. The anti-climactic close — Jesus looked around at everything and left for Bethany because it was late — is, for Bede, Mark's characteristic restraint: no triumphal occupation, no immediate revolution, just the measured advance of the servant-king.

A house of prayer for all nations / a den of robbers — the contrast is total. Isa 56:7 defined the Temple’s universal purpose; Jer 7:11 named its corruption. Jesus uses both texts simultaneously to indict the same institution.
The chief priests and teachers of the law began looking for a way to kill him — the death conspiracy now becomes explicit within the Temple itself. The cleansing has sealed his fate.

Calvin: The crowd’s acclamation of the Davidic kingdom is genuine faith expressing itself in the only categories available. They correctly identify Jesus as the Davidic Messiah; their error is in the kingdom’s content, not its king.

He sat on it — the deliberate fulfilment of Zech 9:9. Calvin notes that Jesus does not announce his messiahship with a speech but with an enacted sign. The sign is legible to those who know the Scripture.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The coming kingdom of our father David — the only place in Mark where the Davidic kingdom is explicitly named in the context of Jesus’s ministry. The crowd’s language is eschatologically charged: they are hailing the restoration of the Davidic era.

Went out to Bethany — Bethany (modern el-Azariyeh, on the eastern slope of Olives) becomes Jesus’s base for Passion Week. He enters the city each day and returns each evening, keeping his lodging outside Jerusalem’s walls.

hist.context: Mark uses his signature sandwich structure: fig tree cursed (Monday morning) / Temple cleansed (Monday) / fig tree withered (Tuesday morning). The fig tree is the parable of the Temple: full of leaves (religious activity) but bearing no fruit (true worship). The authority question is the Temple establishment’s challenge to the one who has just overturned their tables.

sections.header: Verses 12–26 — The Fig Tree; The Temple Cleansed; Authority Questioned

heb.paragraph: My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations — oikos proseukhs. The Temple’s primary purpose — prayer for all nations, not just Israel — is being subverted by the commercial activity in the court of the Gentiles. Jesus’s action restores the space where Gentiles were supposed to pray.

places.name: Bethany
places.coords: Eastern slope of the Mount of Olives; Jesus’s Passion Week base
places.text: Jesus leaves Bethany each morning and returns each evening. The Mount of Olives is both his sleeping ground and the vantage point from which he approaches Jerusalem. The geography of withdrawal and return structures the entire week.

places.name: The Temple courts
places.coords: Jerusalem Temple; specifically the Court of the Gentiles
places.text: The Court of the Gentiles — the outermost court where Gentiles were permitted to pray — had become occupied by the money-changing and dove-selling operations. Jesus’s act restores the Gentile prayer space that the commercial activity had colonized.

tl.name: Monday — Fig tree cursed; Temple cleansed
tl.text: Jesus curses the fruitless fig tree on the Monday walk from Bethany, then drives the traders from the Temple courts.

refs.note: My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations — the text Jesus quotes to define the Temple’s purpose.
Has this house, which bears my Name, become a den of robbers? — Jeremiah’s Temple sermon that Jesus invokes.
What misery is mine! I am like one who gathers summer fruit… not a cluster of grapes… no figs — the OT background for the fruitless fig tree as a prophetic image of covenant failure.

mar.source: Joel Marcus, Mark 1–8 / 8–16, Anchor Bible (2000/2009) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: "It was not the season for figs" — Mark's parenthetical note has generated sustained comment. Marcus argues that the fig tree, like the Temple, is being judged not for failure to fulfil a natural calendar but for false advertising: the leaves signalled the presence of early figs (pāgîm in Hebrew, the small precursor figs that appear with the leaves before the main harvest). A leaf-bearing tree with no early figs was promising what it did not have. The judgment is on the gap between appearance and reality — precisely the Temple's condition.

Marcus: The combination of Isa 56:7 ("house of prayer for all nations") and Jer 7:11 ("den of robbers") is a deliberate double citation. Marcus argues that both texts are being fulfilled simultaneously: Jesus restores the Gentile prayer space (Isa 56's promise) by removing the obstruction (Jer 7's condemnation). The Jer 7 context is critical — Jeremiah's Temple sermon preceded the destruction of 587 BC. The citation imports the full context of coming judgment.

rho.source: David Rhoads & Donald Michie, Mark as Story (3rd ed., 2012) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Rhoads: On 26 — The Fig Tree; The Temple Cleansed; Authority Questioned: iah, the Son of God." The narrator tells the reader what no character in the story yet knows. This creates a sustained dramatic irony — the audience watches characters struggle with a question (who is Jesus?) whose answer has been given in verse 1.

cat.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: The question about John's baptism is, for Chrysostom, a political trap that Jesus turns into a theological mirror. The chief priests and scribes cannot answer because they are not seeking truth — they are seeking rhetorical advantage. Jesus's refusal to answer their question with his own is not evasion but exposure: their silence condemns them. Bede notes that the crowd's opinion ("They feared the people, for everyone held that John really was a prophet") becomes the authorities' constraint — their leadership is entirely reactive to popular sentiment, which is itself a judgment on their fitness to govern.

The authorities' inability to answer Jesus about John's baptism is, for Chrysostom, the definitive exposure of their motivation: they are not seeking truth but managing reputation. Their deliberation — "If we say 'from heaven,' he will ask why we didn't believe him; if we say 'from men'..." — is, for Origen, the anatomy of bad-faith inquiry. Jesus's refusal to answer is not evasion but justice: those who will not answer honestly have no claim on honest answers. Bede notes that this exchange immediately precedes the Parable of the Tenants — the vineyard owners who refuse accountability are already on display in the authorities' non-answer.

MacArthur: On 26 — The Fig Tree; The Temple Cleansed; Authority Questioned: the twin pillars of Mark’s Gospel. Peter’s confession in 8:29 and the centurion’s declaration in 15:39 form an inclusio around the whole book. MacArthur emphasizes the doctrinal significance of this passage within the framework of systematic theology. The text establishes principles that remain normative for the church — not merely descriptive of ancient Israel but prescriptive for how God's people should understand His character and respond in obedience.

Calvin: The fig tree cursed for having leaves but no fruit is the parable of all false religion. The leaves are the external show of piety; the fruit is the genuine transformation of heart and life. The Temple had become all leaves.

By what authority? — Calvin: the establishment’s question is procedurally correct but morally hollow. They want authorization papers, not revelation. Jesus’s counter-question about John’s baptism exposes their proceduralism: they would not accept authority they could not manage.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The court of the Gentiles — not named explicitly in Mark but implied by the Isa 56:7 quotation (“for all nations”). The commercial operations occupied the space designated for Gentile prayer, effectively excluding them from the Temple’s universal purpose.

John’s baptism — from heaven or of human origin? Jesus’s counter-question is not evasion but revelation. If they accepted John’s authority (divine origin), they would recognize Jesus, whom John pointed to. Their refusal to answer exposes their bad faith.

hist.context: Mark uses his signature sandwich structure: fig tree cursed (Monday morning) / Temple cleansed (Monday) / fig tree withered (Tuesday morning). The fig tree is the parable of the Temple: full of leaves (religious activity) but bearing no fruit (true worship). The authority question is the Temple establishment’s challenge to the one who has just overturned their tables.

sections.header: Verses 27–33 — By What Authority? The Baptist’s Witness

heb.paragraph: Hosanna — from the Hebrew hoshiʻa-na: “save now!” It is a cry from Psalm 118:25. What began as a liturgical plea for divine rescue became a shout of messianic welcome. The crowd is greeting the one they believe will bring salvation — but their understanding of what salvation means will be exposed within a week.

places.coords: Villages on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives, c.3km from Jerusalem
places.text: The entry begins on the eastern slope of the Mount of Olives — the traditional approach to Jerusalem from Jericho. Zechariah 14:4 locates the Messiah’s coming on the Mount of Olives. Jesus’s deliberate use of this route is a geographical claim.

places.name: Mount of Olives
places.coords: Hill east of Jerusalem, overlooking the Temple mount across the Kidron Valley
places.text: The Mount of Olives commands a view of the Temple courts. Jesus’s approach from the east, down the olive-covered slope, into the Kidron Valley and up to the Temple, is the route of royal processional. The geography makes the claim visible.

places.coords: The city and its central sanctuary
places.text: Jesus enters the city and goes directly to the Temple — not to Herod’s palace, not to Pilate’s praetorium, but to the house of prayer. The Temple is his destination and his challenge.

tl.name: Triumphal Entry — Palm Sunday
tl.text: Jesus enters Jerusalem on a colt, acclaimed with Psalm 118. He surveys the Temple and withdraws to Bethany. The evaluation has begun.

refs.note: My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations — the text Jesus quotes to define the Temple’s purpose.
Has this house, which bears my Name, become a den of robbers? — Jeremiah’s Temple sermon that Jesus invokes.
What misery is mine! I am like one who gathers summer fruit… not a cluster of grapes… no figs — the OT background for the fruitless fig tree as a prophetic image of covenant failure.

mar.source: Joel Marcus,Mark 1–8; Mark 8–16(Word Biblical Commentary) — Faithful Paraphrase

notes.note: Marcus: On 33 — By What Authority? The Baptist’s Witness: omposition" well attested in Second Temple Jewish midrash. The Qumran community (1QS 8:12-14) also applied Isa 40:3 to their own wilderness mission, suggesting that this text was a live messianic-preparatory image in Jesus's era.

cat.source: Thomas Aquinas (compiler), Catena Aurea — Patristic Commentary, Public Domain

notes.note: Patristic commentary on 33 — By What Authority? The Baptist’s Witness:  action. The quotation from Isaiah and Malachi (vv.2-3) in one breath is deliberate: Aquinas, following Bede, says it shows the two offices of the Forerunner — as the messenger of the covenant (Malachi) and as the voice in the wilderness (Isaiah). The prophecy precedes the person; the word precedes the Word.

Calvin: On 33 — By What Authority? The Baptist’s Witness: ng — because the gospel is primarily the announcement of salvation. The prophetic testimony grounds it in ancient covenant fulfilment, not new invention. Calvin's reading characteristically emphasizes divine sovereignty and human responsibility in tension — God ordains the outcome while the actors remain morally accountable. This dual emphasis reflects Calvin's broader hermeneutic: Scripture always speaks both about God's plan and about human duty under that plan.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: On 33 — By What Authority? The Baptist’s Witness: ingle event at the end. The wild animals are unique to Mark — possibly Edenic restoration imagery or Markan geographical realism about the desert terrain. The NET translators note that the underlying Hebrew/Greek syntax carries nuances that most English versions cannot fully represent. Lexical analysis of the key terms reveals semantic ranges that enrich the passage beyond what a single English gloss can convey, connecting this text to a wider web of biblical usage.

hist.context: Mark uses his signature sandwich structure: fig tree cursed (Monday morning) / Temple cleansed (Monday) / fig tree withered (Tuesday morning). The fig tree is the parable of the Temple: full of leaves (religious activity) but bearing no fruit (true worship). The authority question is the Temple establishment’s challenge to the one who has just overturned their tables.

ppl.name: The chief priests and teachers of the law
ppl.text: From 11:18 onward, the chief priests and teachers of the law are the primary human forces moving toward Jesus’s death. Their authority challenge (11:27-33) begins the series of Temple controversies in chs.12-13.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Is it not written: 'My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations'? But you have made it 'a den of robbers.'"ESV"Is it not written, 'My house shall be called a house of prayer for all the nations'? But you have made it a den of robbers."KJV"Is it not written, My house shall be called of all nations the house of prayer? but ye have made it a den of thieves."NASB"Is it not written: 'My house will be called a house of prayer for all the nations'? But you have made it a robbers' den."NLT"The Scriptures declare, 'My Temple will be called a house of prayer for all nations,' but you have turned it into a den of thieves."

src.title: Josephus, Antiquities 15.380-425
src.quote: Josephus describes Herod’s Temple complex in detail, including the Court of the Gentiles and the commercial activity associated with the festival crowds.
src.note: Confirms the historical context of the money-changing and animal-selling operations that Jesus confronted.

src.title: Mishnah Sheqalim 1:3
src.quote: Rabbinic text describing the money-changing operations at the Temple — changing foreign currency into Tyrian shekels (required for the Temple tax).
src.note: Confirms that the money-changing was an established, authorized institution, making Jesus’s disruption of it a challenge to official Temple practice.

rec.text: Sanders argued that the Temple action was not a “cleansing” but a symbolic act of destruction — a prophetic sign that the Temple was under divine judgment. The overturned tables signified the Temple’s coming overthrow (13:2).

rec.who: N.T. Wright, Jesus and the Victory of God (1996)
rec.text: Wright argued that Jesus was acting as the true Messiah claiming his Father’s house, enacting Mal 3:1-3 and Isa 56:7 simultaneously. The action was both prophetic judgment and messianic claim.

rows.label: The Markan Sandwich: Fig Tree and Temple
Authority Questioned: The Week’s Central Challenge

hebtext.note: Hosanna — from the Hebrew hoshiʻa-na: “save now!” It is a cry from Psalm 118:25. What began as a liturgical plea for divine rescue became a shout of messianic welcome. The crowd is greeting the one they believe will bring salvation — but their understanding of what salvation means will be exposed within a week.

My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations — oikos proseukhs. The Temple’s primary purpose — prayer for all nations, not just Israel — is being subverted by the commercial activity in the court of the Gentiles. Jesus’s action restores the space where Gentiles were supposed to pray.

Hosanna — from the Hebrew hoshiʻa-na: “save now!” It is a cry from Psalm 118:25. What began as a liturgical plea for divine rescue became a shout of messianic welcome. The crowd is greeting the one they believe will bring salvation — but their understanding of what salvation means will be exposed within a week.

tx.title: "The Lord needs it" — kyrios as Jesus or as God
tx.content: Kyrios (Lord) could refer to Jesus himself — an early christological usage — or could mean "its owner/master" in a secular sense. No textual variant; the ambiguity is semantic.
tx.note: The deliberate ambiguity between divine title and ordinary owner designation is characteristic of Mark's understated Christology. Both readings are simultaneously possible.

tx.title: Verse 11:26 — omitted in modern editions
tx.content: Sinaiticus, Vaticanusomit 11:26 entirely;later MSSadd "But if you do not forgive, neither will your Father in heaven forgive your trespasses" (harmonising with Matt 6:15).
tx.note: Almost certainly a scribal addition. No reliable early MS includes it. The NIV omits it or brackets it in a footnote.

debate.title: Was the Temple action a cleansing or a judgment?

positions.name: Cleansing view
positions.proponents: Jesus restored the Temple’s proper function by removing commercial corruption from the Court of the Gentiles. The action is reformist.

positions.name: Judgment view
positions.proponents: Jesus enacted the Temple’s coming destruction. The overturned tables are a prophetic sign, not a reform measure. E.P. Sanders argues this view.

themes.note: Mark 11 is the Temple chapter: Jesus enters, surveys, cleanses, and provokes the authority challenge. Every element of the chapter orbits the Temple as the site of the covenant’s climactic crisis.

coaching.tip: The triumphal entry fulfills Zechariah 9:9: a king on a donkey. Humble royalty. The crowds shout Psalm 118; within days they'll cry 'Crucify!' Hosannas fade fast.
The fig tree cursed, the temple cleansed—a sandwich structure. The fruitless tree interprets the fruitless temple. Both bear leaves but no fruit; both face judgment.

prayer_prompt: As Jesus curses the fig tree that has leaves but no fruit, consider asking God whether your spiritual life looks impressive from a distance but is producing nothing that nourishes others.
